original_hypothesis.title: Inconsistent Ordering of setConfirmedRival and setConfirmed Between Confirmation Paths
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: confirmEdgeByOneStepProof calls setConfirmedRival BEFORE setConfirmed, while confirmEdgeByTime calls setConfirmed BEFORE setConfirmedRival. This inconsistency means that in confirmEdgeByOneStepProof, if setConfirmedRival passes but setConfirmed reverts (edge not Pending), the confirmed rival record is set but the edge status remains Pending. This could create an inconsistent state where confirmedRivals points to a non-confirmed edge.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: State consistency between confirmedRivals and edge status is critical. If confirmedRivals[mutualId] points to a Pending edge, no rival can ever be confirmed for that mutualId (setConfirmedRival reverts), permanently blocking challenge resolution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setConfirmedRival(store, edgeId) called on line 515 BEFORE setConfirmed on line 518; in confirmEdgeByTime (line 464-465) the order is reversed

preconditions: An edge at the SmallStep level exists
The edge is somehow not in Pending status when confirmEdgeByOneStepProof is called
The edge has no confirmed rival yet
All other checks in confirmEdgeByOneStepProof pass before setConfirmedRival

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: setConfirmedRival before setConfirmed; if setConfirmed reverts, rival record already set
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Solidity reverts are all-or-nothing within a transaction, so within a single tx this is atomic. BUT if the code is ever called via try/catch or delegatecall context, partial state changes could persist.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Ordering should be setConfirmed then setConfirmedRival for consistency

analysis_questions: Since both operations are in the same transaction, does the Solidity revert semantics prevent partial state?
Could any future upgrade introduce try/catch around this code?
Is there any delegatecall context where partial state persistence is possible?
validation_requirements: Verify that transaction atomicity prevents the inconsistent state
Confirm that no external call exists between setConfirmedRival and setConfirmed

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Under normal Solidity semantics, if setConfirmed reverts, the entire transaction reverts (including setConfirmedRival). However, the code inconsistency suggests a design error. If the contract is ever called through a context that catches reverts (e.g., try/catch in an upgrade), setConfirmedRival could persist without setConfirmed, permanently blocking challenge resolution for that mutualId.
prerequisites: Edge somehow not in Pending when reaching setConfirmed

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent blocking of challenge resolution for affected mutualId group

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing ordering of confirmation operations between the two paths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare confirmedRivals state with edge status

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
